items.mistake: Committing 'fix everything' — 20 unrelated changes in one commit.
items.fix: Stage selectively (`git add -p`) and make one commit per coherent change.
items.why: Fat commits are unreviewable, unbisectable and impossible to partially revert.
items.example:
```bash
git add -p src/auth.py        # pick only the auth-related hunks
git commit -m 'Add login endpoint'
git add README.md
git commit -m 'Fix typo'
```

items.mistake: Committing secrets, large binaries or generated files.
items.fix: Add a thorough `.gitignore` *before* the first commit; use `git-secrets` or pre-commit hooks for credentials.
items.why: History is forever — even a deleted secret stays in the log unless you rewrite history across every fork.

items.fix: Always cut a feature branch first: `git switch -c feature/<short-name>`.
items.why: Direct commits on `main` skip review, block other people's merges, and tangle history.
items.example:
```bash
git switch main && git pull
git switch -c feature/improve-prompt
```

items.fix: Use the imperative voice ('Add X', 'Fix Y') and explain *why* in the body.
items.why: Commit messages are the documentation your future self relies on during `git blame` / `bisect`.
items.example:
```
Add retry on 429 from embedding API

The upstream rate-limits us at ~1 req/s; previously we crashed the
batch. Adds exponential backoff capped at 60s.
```

items.mistake: Amending or force-pushing a branch others are working on.
items.fix: Amend / force-push only on your own un-shared branches. On shared branches, create a follow-up commit.
items.why: Force-push rewrites history — collaborators' clones get out of sync and they lose work.
items.example:
```bash
# Only safe BEFORE push or on a private branch:
git commit --amend --no-edit
git push --force-with-lease   # safer than --force
```
